paragraphs.text: RYANAIR
Ref. Arps(2025)9124387 - 24/10/2025 Ryanair Dublin Office Airside Business Park Swords County Dublin Ireland Telephone: +353 1 945 1212 Website: www.ryanair.com
24 October 2025
Ryanair’s feedback on the Digital Fairness Act
Executive Summary

evidence_quotes.quote: guidance from the Commission on the existing legislation, coupled with more robust enforcement measures.

paragraphs.text: Ryanair welcomes the European Commission’s initiative to review the EU consumer law framework, however as a preliminary and overarching comment, we echo the EU leaders in their letter to President Costa on 20 October 2025, in calling for “self-restraint when it comes to new legislation, which should anyway come with strengthened impact assessments.” The aviation industry in particular is already a heavily regulated sector, and while we absolutely welcome the Commission’s dedication to strengthening consumer protection, and in particular using transparency as a mechanism to do so, we would respectfully say that a lot of the problematic practices as identified within the impact assessment are capable of being addressed using existing legislation. As such, we believe that what is needed to strengthen consumer protection and address digital fairness is not more (potentially duplicative) legislation but rather, guidance from the Commission on the existing legislation, coupled with more robust enforcement measures.

As Europe’s largest airline, Ryanair has long supported measures that strengthen consumer protection, provided they are proportionate, evidence-based, and applied consistently across all market participants. Transparency is a fundamental tenet of Ryanair’s business model and as such, permeates throughout our business practices, the manner in which we engage with consumers and throughout our partnerships. However, we remain concerned that existing enforcement gaps continue to expose consumers to misleading practices while penalising compliant operators.

Ryanair fully supports the Commission’s objective to tackle “dark patterns”, unfair personalisation, misleading marketing, and deceptive pricing. Any new measures, however, should safeguard consumers without undermining innovation, freedom of enterprise, or the competitive diversity that has made EU air transport a global success story.
The importance of liberalisation and innovation for the EU air transport market

The liberalisation of the EU air transport market under Regulations (EEC) No 2407/92, 2408/92, 2409/92, and subsequently Regulation (EC) No 1008/2008, transformed air travel into a truly democratic mode of transport. By granting airlines the freedom to set fares and separate optional services from the core transport service (Articles 22 and 23 of Regulation (EC) No 1008/2008), what was once a privilege reserved for the wealthy is now a widely available means of mass transport.

This liberalised model has delivered significant benefits: affordable connectivity across Europe, market-driven innovation, and enhanced consumer choice. Ryanair’s growth across all EU Member States reflects the enduring success of the EU’s open skies policy and its contribution to economic integration and regional cohesion.

In a highly competitive digital environment, innovation in user experience and service design is essential. Airlines invest significantly in digital tools that enhance transparency, simplify choices, and provide consumers with relevant information in an intuitive manner.

As underlined in the Draghi Report on European Competitiveness (2024), the EU must now focus on policies that reduce regulatory complexity, enhance efficiency, and promote scale and innovation, rather than introducing further overlapping or prescriptive rules. The Draghi Report emphasises the need for less, not more, regulation in the aviation sector. It highlights that additional regulatory burdens risk stifling competition and innovation, and advocates preserving the flexible, open framework that underpins market growth and consumer benefit.

Board of Directors: E Brennan, R Brennan, R Conway, E Daly, G Doherty, B Grabowski (FR), E Köstinger (AT), J Laghrari Laabi (MA), S McCarthy – Chairman, A Nolan, M O'Leary, A Rudd (UK) Registered Office: Ryanair Dublin Office, Airside Business Park, Swords, County Dublin, Ireland Company No. 104547 | VAT No. 4749148U | Designated Activity Company

The Draghi Report rightly underscores the urgent need for the European Union to enhance its global competitiveness. In this context, it is essential that EU legislators exercise caution when introducing new consumer protection measures. Legislation that unnecessarily or unreasonably disadvantages businesses risks undermining the integrity and dynamism of the EU internal market. More importantly, such measures may inadvertently harm the very consumers they are intended to protect—by reducing choice, increasing costs, or stifling innovation. A balanced approach is therefore critical to ensure that consumer interests are safeguarded without compromising the EU's economic resilience and global standing.

Simplification and deregulation remain key to sustaining the EU's dynamic air transport ecosystem. Europe risks falling behind global competitors unless it prioritises productivity, investment, and competitiveness over regulatory accumulation.

evidence_quotes.quote: the right of companies to determine how to present their products and services.

paragraphs.text: The freedom to conduct a business, protected under Article 16 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union, includes the right of companies to determine how to present their products and services. Any new restrictions must therefore be contextual, proportionate, and technologically neutral, ensuring that legitimate commercial design and innovation are not unjustly constrained.

evidence_quotes.quote: Visual cues such as colour highlighting are standard branding strategies to increase usability and clarity.

paragraphs.text: In the air travel industry, diversification of carriers' offerings is associated with measurable benefits for consumers. Visual cues such as colour highlighting are standard branding strategies to increase usability and clarity. For airlines, these tools help passengers compare service packages efficiently, enhancing transparency rather than misleading consumers. An airline's ability to utilise such branding strategies is incredibly important. Recommending certain products to passengers adds significant value to the passenger's overall travel experience. If airlines are in some way prevented from branding in a manner which recommends genuinely advantageous fare bundles, this will only serve to stifle innovation.

Continuous development of technology for differentiation, evaluation, and adaptation is a prerequisite for success. These innovations improve the user experience, operational efficiency, and competitiveness in a rapidly evolving digital environment.

Air travel involves complex, demand-based pricing, unbundled services, and multiple consumer considerations. Ryanair strongly supports the Commission's objectives to enhance transparency, accountability, and consumer protection online.

However, it is essential that the Commission acknowledges aviation's particularities and does not inadvertently penalise "bright practices" - pro-consumer digital innovations that empower passengers with better information, improved choice, and fairer prices.
In aviation, "bright practices" include:

evidence_quotes.quote: Clearly communicating advantages of direct booking channels, including flexibility options and direct customer service;

paragraphs.text: Clearly communicating advantages of direct booking channels, including flexibility options and direct customer service;
Prominently displaying upgraded product features, allowing passengers to compare value beyond price;
Providing transparent prompts and recommendations for ancillary services (e.g., seat selection, baggage, insurance);
Stimulating off-peak travel demand through digital engagement, promotional campaigns, and dynamic pricing;

Maintaining non-personalised, demand-based pricing that reflects load factor, historic route performance, booking timing, and seasonality, ensuring efficient capacity use and lower average fares without individual profiling.
Ryanair recommends that the Commission:
Define "bright practices" and specifically categorise them as compliant, pro-consumer innovations.

evidence_quotes.quote: Ensure transparency obligations do not restrict airlines' ability to highlight benefits of enhanced offerings.

paragraphs.text: Ensure transparency obligations do not restrict airlines' ability to highlight benefits of enhanced offerings.
Promote sector-specific guidance reflecting aviation's unique regulatory and operational context.
Unfair Commercial Practices related to dark patterns

evidence_quotes.quote: deceptive user interface designs purposely conceived to manipulate consumer choice.

paragraphs.text: Ryanair strongly supports the Commission’s focus on combating deceptive user interface designs purposely conceived to manipulate consumer choice. We have for several years called upon regulators to prevent certain intermediaries from deploying those very practices on unsuspecting consumers. We, however, respectfully say that these practices are already robustly addressed within the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive 2005/29/EC which specifically prohibits misleading practices which would cause the average consumer to take a transactional decision that he would not have otherwise taken.

evidence_quotes.quote: significant hidden mark-ups to airline fares and ancillary services
purported “discounts” on those fares if consumers sign up to their subscription service
the “discount” applied is, as a matter of fact, always significantly less than the intermediary advertises.
sign up to their subscription service

paragraphs.text: As a working example, we have reported a particular intermediary who operates a subscription model to several regulators because they engage in a practice whereby they systematically apply significant hidden mark-ups to airline fares and ancillary services and then offer purported “discounts” on those fares if consumers sign up to their subscription service. Because they apply significant hidden mark-ups to the true price of the airline fares and ancillary services, the “discount” applied is, as a matter of fact, always significantly less than the intermediary advertises. Where a consumer does not subscribe to the subscription service, they will always be subject to significant hidden mark-ups and where they do subscribe to the service they will always be subject to false representations in relation to the amount of “discount” they have in fact received. The net effect of this is that the intermediary’s material omissions in respect of the true price of airline fares and ancillary services cause the average consumer to take a transactional decision that they would not have otherwise taken.

evidence_quotes.quote: Based on the Commission’s proposed definition of dark patterns

paragraphs.text: Based on the Commission’s proposed definition of dark patterns (“unfair commercial practices deployed through the structure, design or functionalities of digital interfaces or system architecture that can influence consumers to make decisions they would not have taken otherwise”), it is clear that that particular intermediary’s deceptive practices fall within the proposed definition of “dark patterns”, but yet, the practices can be adequately addressed by simply invoking the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive 2005/29/EC.

evidence_quotes.quote: anti-consumer “dark patterns” that exploit passengers seeking flight disruption compensation

paragraphs.text: Similarly, EU261 claims management companies have been criticised for employing anti-consumer “dark patterns” that exploit passengers seeking flight disruption compensation. These practices include:
Opaque fee structures, where “no win, no fee” models often conceal deductions of up to 59% or more from successful claims;

evidence_quotes.quote: misrepresent the ease of filing claims directly with airlines
pressuring consumers into unnecessary third-party contracts

paragraphs.text: Aggressive marketing tactics that misrepresent the ease of filing claims directly with airlines, pressuring consumers into unnecessary third-party contracts;

evidence_quotes.quote: Limited process transparency, with passengers frequently unable to track claim progress or access timely updates;

paragraphs.text: Limited process transparency, with passengers frequently unable to track claim progress or access timely updates;

evidence_quotes.quote: intermediaries divert a significant portion of EU261 payouts

paragraphs.text: Reduced compensation outcomes, as intermediaries divert a significant portion of EU261 payouts, undermining the regulation’s consumer-protection intent.

evidence_quotes.quote: leveraging digital design and information gaps

paragraphs.text: These practices already are in breach of the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive and the Consumer Rights Directive. In both cases, these intermediaries exploit regulatory asymmetry – leveraging digital design and information gaps to insert themselves between airlines and passengers. The result is a degraded consumer experience and distorted market outcomes, contrary to the Commission’s objectives to ensure digital fairness.
We would therefore respectfully call upon the Commission to consider the existing legislation and how it may be utilised prior to creating a potentially duplicative body of legislation.
Ryanair urges the Commission to issue guidance on existing legislation to tackle systemic abuses by intermediaries, and such guidance should:

evidence_quotes.quote: full disclosure of intermediary service fees

paragraphs.text: Require full disclosure of intermediary service fees at every stage of the booking or claims process;
Mandate clear separation between airline fares and intermediary-imposed surcharges;

evidence_quotes.quote: Prohibit brand mimicry or misleading interface design that falsely implies official airline partnership;

paragraphs.text: Prohibit brand mimicry or misleading interface design that falsely implies official airline partnership;
Ensure transparency obligations apply equally to all digital intermediaries – including claims management firms – whose business models rely on consumer manipulation rather than service value.

evidence_quotes.quote: manipulative or exploitative targeting must be prohibited

paragraphs.text: While manipulative or exploitative targeting must be prohibited, the future framework must not undermine legitimate, non-personalised, demand-based pricing models that are essential to efficient and affordable air travel in Europe. In aviation, fares are determined by objective, non-personal factors such as load factor, booking time, seasonality, and route performance. These transparent and data-driven systems enable airlines to optimise seat availability, stimulate demand during off-peak periods, and keep fares competitive for all consumers.

To eliminate manipulative digital practices, regulatory obligations must be clearly defined. Regulatory obligations that are overly broad or prescriptive could unintentionally stifle innovation and flexibility in user interface design or fail to accommodate diverse consumer needs, leading to a one-size-fits-all approach.
To ensure clarity, proportionality, and consumer benefit, Ryanair suggests the Commission should:
Differentiate between exploitative personalisation and lawful, non-personalised dynamic pricing, recognising that the latter supports lower average fares and fair access;
Acknowledge sector-specific pricing models in aviation as transparent, evidence-based, and pro-competitive;
Ground any design-related obligations in clear, evidence-based criteria, focusing on demonstrably harmful practices rather than broad mandates;
Avoid blanket restrictions that could limit access to affordable travel or reduce the effectiveness of digital channels as tools for transparency and consumer choice.

Recognising both the distinction between manipulative personalisation and legitimate demand-based pricing, and the need for clear, proportionate guidance on interface design, will protect consumers without constraining innovation or Europe’s successful low-fare model.

The Commission has explicitly highlighted potential consumer concerns in the event ticket sector, particularly regarding dynamic pricing and resale practices, which doesn’t apply at all to the aviation industry. The aviation sector is entirely different and enjoys full price transparency, whereas the event ticket sector makes customers wait in queues for hours without knowing the price of the ticket and then pressurises customers into making a purchase in a short time frame. In any event, this can be addressed by relying on existing legislation. While this sector illustrates risks such as sudden price spikes, opaque fees, or misleading expectations, the underlying issue is transparency and consumer understanding, not the lawful practice of price variation itself. Ryanair fully supports this.
In aviation, dynamic pricing is fundamentally different:
“Demand based pricing” is a more accurate term in the aviation industry;

evidence_quotes.quote: Fares vary gradually and predictably based on objective, non-personal factors, including seat availability, advance booking timing, seasonality, and route-wide demand trends;

paragraphs.text: Fares vary gradually and predictably based on objective, non-personal factors, including seat availability, advance booking timing, seasonality, and route-wide demand trends;

evidence_quotes.quote: Consumers are always presented with the current price at every stage of the booking process, meaning passengers are always presented with the current price at the point of decision with full visibility of fare components and associated conditions;

paragraphs.text: Consumers are always presented with the current price at every stage of the booking process, meaning passengers are always presented with the current price at the point of decision with full visibility of fare components and associated conditions;

evidence_quotes.quote: Prices are not artificially locked or spiked

paragraphs.text: Prices are not artificially locked or spiked, and all passengers are treated equally, ensuring fairness.
These practices ensure efficient seat allocation, encourage early booking, and maintain low-fare access for European passengers.

Considering this, we respectfully say that it is wholly impractical to attempt to regulate dynamic pricing using the same body of legislation across all sectors. To do so would entirely ignore what has become industry standard within the airline industry and which, if anything, is pro-consumer, as the deployment of demand based pricing in highly competitive, high-volume services with continuous availability (like air services) ensures the availability of low fares for passengers and ultimately passengers paying only what they are willing to pay. This is, in our respectful submission, what should be considered a “Bright Practice”.

evidence_quotes.quote: dynamic pricing is deployed in entirely different ways across different sectors
carve out the airline industry as demand based pricing is an integral part of how airlines in particular price their services

paragraphs.text: A failure to recognise that dynamic pricing is deployed in entirely different ways across different sectors would be detrimental to the airline industry. Ryanair therefore requests that if the Commission is minded to regulate dynamic pricing in the manner suggested, that it carve out the airline industry as demand based pricing is an integral part of how airlines in particular price their services, it is not harmful to consumers, and if anything it guarantees low fare access for European passengers.
Ryanair requests that the Commission :
Focus on transparency and predictability of price dynamics;
Recognise sector-specific dynamic pricing as lawful and pro-consumer, in line with Regulation (EC) No 1008/2008;
Encourage clear communication about how prices may change, enabling consumers to make informed decisions without constraining lawful market mechanisms.

evidence_quotes.quote: transparent, gradual, and fully observable pricing

paragraphs.text: By focusing on transparency and acknowledging transparent, gradual, and fully observable pricing, rather than the mere fact of price variation, the Commission can protect consumers while supporting innovative, fair, and competitive pricing in the European aviation sector.
The current fragmentation of enforcement across Member States allows certain intermediaries to exploit jurisdictional gaps and creates inconsistencies and difficulties for traders' compliance.
Ryanair supports:
Requiring intermediaries facilitating consumer transactions to identify their contractual counterparties clearly and ensure access to applicable terms;
Promoting consistent interpretation of Articles 5–7 of the Consumer Rights Directive, ensuring transparency in contract formation and digital content supply.

Ryanair reiterates its support for the European Commission’s objective to ensure digital fairness and consumer trust. However, this can only be achieved through balanced regulation and effective enforcement. We therefore request that prior to proposing an entirely new body of legislation, the Commission consider the existing legislation (in line with the Draghi Report and the EU leaders’ letter to President Costo) to establish if there are certain aspects of the proposed Digital Fairness Act which are already governed by existing legislation.
In addition, we request that any proposed Digital Fairness Act and accompanying legislative amendments:
Ensure proportionality and technological neutrality in defining dark patterns and design constraints.

evidence_quotes.quote: “bright practices” that enhance transparency, choice, and value

paragraphs.text: Differentiate legitimate business models and “bright practices” that enhance transparency, choice, and value, from exploitative practices;
Uphold the freedom to conduct a business under Article 16 of the Charter as a guiding principle in all regulatory design.
Recognise industry particularities and aviation-specific regulation as taking precedence, avoiding both regulatory overlapping and unnecessary burdens intended for higher-risk industries.
Preserve sector-specific innovation, transparency, and consumer choice, particularly regarding unbundled fares and demand-based pricing.

Airlines like Ryanair have pioneered transparent pricing, direct customer communication, and user-friendly booking interfaces that promote genuine consumer empowerment. These innovations – which include demand based pricing, transparent ancillary options, and sustainability information – should be recognised and protected, not constrained under a one-size-fits-all interpretation of digital fairness.

Ryanair remains fully committed to engaging constructively with the European Commission and other stakeholders as the Digital Fairness Act evolves. We welcome further dialogue to ensure that future consumer law reforms promote fairness, transparency, and genuine consumer benefit across the European aviation sector.
The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.
